looking for a way to add custom js callbacks during move/drag events with the PointDrawTool. There was a discussion about this on [Discourse](https://discourse.bokeh.org/t/change-when-pointdrawtool-syncs/7654).

The situation is that I'd like to update data on one plot, while the data on another plot is getting moved. Here is a simplified example app:
```python
# file is main.py within a "simple-pointdraw" directory.
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource, PointDrawTool, CustomJS
from bokeh.plotting import figure
from bokeh.io import curdoc
from bokeh.layouts import row, layout

left = figure(x_range=(0, 10), y_range=(0, 10), tools=[],
           title='Point Draw Tool')
left.background_fill_color = 'lightgrey'
right = figure(x_range=(0, 10), y_range=(0, 10), tools=[],
           title='Synced Plot')
right.background_fill_color = 'lightgrey'

source = ColumnDataSource({
    'x': [1, 5, 9],
    'y': [1, 5, 9],
    'y2': [2, 6, 10],
    'color': ['red', 'green', 'yellow'],
})

js = """
for (var i = 0; i<cb_obj.data['y'].length; i++) {
	var y = cb_obj.data['y'][i];
	cb_obj.data['y2'][i] = y + 1;
}
cb_obj.change.emit();
"""
source.js_on_change('data', CustomJS(code=js))

left_renderer = left.scatter(x='x', y='y', source=source, color='color', size=10)
right_renderer = right.scatter(x='x', y='y2', source=source, color='color', size=10)

draw_tool = PointDrawTool(renderers=[left_renderer], empty_value='black')

left.add_tools(draw_tool)
left.toolbar.active_tap = draw_tool

curdoc().add_root(row(left, right))
curdoc().title = "Synced Plots"
```
The modified y-values in the callback don't get updated until the movement is complete:
![bokeh-move-pts](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/997120/115614157-2c813900-a2a2-11eb-98a4-91244860a21f.gif)

This example is fairly simple and could probably be achieved with [CustomJSTransform](https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/user_guide/data.html?highlight=customjstransform#including-javascript-code-with-customjstransform), but my exact use case is more complicated and would require a [custom tool](https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/user_guide/extensions_gallery/tool.html) I had an issue like this. This may or may not be related here, as the questions asks about a pure JS solution. Nonetheless, I found that instead of doing: 

```python
...
source.on_change("data", ...)
```

where `source` is the data source for the PointDrawTool, one can instead use: 

```python
def on_mouse_move(event):
    selected_index = source.selected.indices
    if len(selected_index) > 0: 
        # detection & change logic 
        
fig.on_event("mousemove", on_mouse_move)
```

For example, you can update all the coordinates mentioned by the OP using the mouse event coordinates in both figures. When the drag event ends, the `selected.indices` reverts to the empty list, and the final source.on_change('data', ...) callback triggers to finalize the movement. 

Again this might be irrelevant for the OP, who wanted a pure JS solution.

issue mostly pertains to the contents of `bokeh.models`)

Our reference docs are currently hand-curated into topical groupings. The original reason for the the groupings was that early on we had issues supporting and effective search capability, and because the API surface was much smaller and easier to organize by hand. Search has since gotten better and the API surface has grown considerably.

In addition to posing a manual maintenance burden, this organization also results in som very heavy pages in our docs. These large page sizes can cause problems or block new features:

* https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/13799
* https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/12707

I think we must split the API docs into a one-class-per-page organization. I.e. instead of:

```
https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/reference/models/tools.html#bokeh.models.TapTool
```

there would be a dedicated page like:
```
https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/reference/models/TapTool
```

This organization is common in other projects and is supported by some tools e.g. [`autoapi`](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) and others. It will take some research and experimentation to see whether these tools can be used "out of the box" or if we will need to make other changes to accommodate them or (worst-case) develop our our tooling. I'll try to do some minimal investigation to see how/if existing tools currently function.

There are some questions that probably intersect with this, such as the idea to split up the monolithic `bokeh.models` namespace itself at the user-facing API level (cc @mattpap I thought there was an issue/discussion about this but I can't find it)

cc @bokeh/dev for comments (not triaging as "discussion" since I think the necessity of change is making itself evident)

Take, for example, the RangeTool vs the TapTool docs. Here's [a diff of their docs](https://www.diffchecker.com/Odh4fp3P/): of the 465 lines of the line constituting TapTools docs, 431 of them are inherited boilerplate shared with RangeTool (and many others). 

In other words, if I want to read about what `TapTool` does and how it differs from `RangeTool` (say), I have to explicitly scan over / ignore 93% of the documentation to find the information I want. This is quite cumbersome! 

A single-page-per-class could help here, but actually one of the real culprits is the massive inheritance trees! This sounds like maybe a limitation of the sphinx pipeline you're using. Maybe an issue should be raised somewhere else regarding this? 

> For example: going to the ColumnDataSouce page and not finding an entry for selection (because it happens to be defined on the obscure DataSource base) would be bad.

I guess this is a bit subjective. Though argument forwarding (à la `*args` and `**kwargs`) is somewhat common in Python, I admit it's not as ubiquitous as it is in e.g. R (or when it happens, it's only one level deep). I personally have no problems tracing up the inheritance hierarchy to understand the configurability of the API, though I admit the vast majority of the projects I work with (e.g. numpy & scipy) don't have _near_ the level of inheritance as bokeh. 

I do think inherited member should at the very least be treated differently. Even a simple fix like a collapsible (which is already done for the JSON prototypes) would do wonders, like: 


## TapTool 
- mode
- behavior
- gesture
- modifiers
- ...etc  
<details>
<summary> Inherited </summary>
- icon
- id 
- js_event_callbacks
- etc. 
</details>

Describe your issue.

It seems `LinearOperator`'s are not entirely compatible with ARPACK's shift-invert mode?

I can produce instances where estimating the smallest eigenvalue of a sparse, symmetric, positive-definite matrix works fine when the input is an `spmatrix`, but fails when a `LinearOperator` is given. 

Here's an small minimal example: 
```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.sparse.linalg import eigsh
from scipy.sparse import csc_array

# Small, connected graph Laplacian (psd)
L = csc_array([
    [ 2., -1.,  0.,  0., -1.],
    [-1.,  2.,  0., -1.,  0.],
    [ 0.,  0.,  1., -1.,  0.],
    [ 0., -1., -1.,  3., -1.],
    [-1.,  0.,  0., -1.,  2.]
])

```

The above matrix is a graph Laplacian derived from a random 5-vertex connected graph. Since the graph is connected, the rank (nullity) should be 4 (1). Thus, the smallest eigenvalue should be quite close to 0.  This works for these two examples: 

```python
eigsh(L, k=1, which='SM', return_eigenvectors=False)
# -1.11108845e-16 
eigsh(L, k=1, sigma=0, which='LM', return_eigenvectors=False)
# 8.8817842e-17
```

But this fails for the matrix converted to a linear operator. 
```python
eigsh(aslinearoperator(L), k=1, sigma=0, which='LM', return_eigenvectors=False)
# Error in inverting M: function gmres_loose did not converge (info = 50).
```

I initially thought maybe the problem was that `L` is singular, since shift-invert mode does require an inverse operator (which is handled internally). But adding a decent amount of mass to diagonal to make the operator positive definite does not seem help: 

```python
eigsh(L+(1e-4)*np.eye(L.shape[0]), k=1, sigma=0, which='LM', return_eigenvectors=False)
# 0.0001 
eigsh(aslinearoperator(L+(1e-4)*np.eye(L.shape[0])), k=1, sigma=0, which='LM', return_eigenvectors=False)
# Error in inverting M: function gmres_loose did not converge (info = 50).
```

What am I missing? 


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.sparse.linalg import eigsh
from scipy.sparse import csc_array

# Small, connected graph Laplacian (psd)
L = csc_array([
    [ 2., -1.,  0.,  0., -1.],
    [-1.,  2.,  0., -1.,  0.],
    [ 0.,  0.,  1., -1.,  0.],
    [ 0., -1., -1.,  3., -1.],
    [-1.,  0.,  0., -1.,  2.]
])


eigsh(L, k=1, sigma=0, which='LM', return_eigenvectors=False)
eigsh(aslinearoperator(L), k=1, sigma=0, which='LM', return_eigenvectors=False)
```

